evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/newship.go` composes low-level infrastructure callbacks (docker orchestration, volumes, container lifecycle, config mutation, event publication) inside a ship-domain workflow entrypoint, blurring domain and infrastructure ownership.

`goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go` combines message action dispatch, drive resolution, patp validation, event-side effects, and async provisioning kickoff in one package surface, making top-level role boundaries harder to explain.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go` and `container_bridge.go` together act as a container platform adapter layer but are exposed as generic orchestration package responsibilities.
The reviewed files mostly rely on shared internal packages rather than introducing overlapping third-party libraries for the same concern.

Docker interactions are routed through project-local seams/runtime bundles in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` and `goseg/docker/network/network.go`, reducing direct package-level fan-out from callers.

Global mutable default runtimes (`DefaultEventRuntime`, default broadcast runtime/controller, default docker subsystem runtime) create implicit runtime coupling even when compile-time imports are clean.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Runtime wiring still depends on mutable package-level singletons, which increases hidden coupling and complicates dependency reasoning under tests and long-lived processes.

evidence: `goseg/importer/importer.go` and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go` define very large callback-bag runtimes split across many embedded structs; integration behavior is spread across many function fields and validation groups.

`goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` and `goseg/importer/importer.go` both capture `events.DefaultEventRuntime()` during runtime construction, coupling orchestration seams to mutable global event brokers.
`goseg/docker/events/events.go` allows swapping the default runtime at runtime (`SetDefaultEventRuntime`), so producers/consumers created at different times can silently target different channels.
`goseg/defaults/defaults.go` uses a stringly-typed selector (`DockerData(pathType string)`) with branch-on-literal behavior; invalid values silently fall into the non-volume branch.

Callers in `goseg/defaults/defaults.go` and `goseg/config/runtimecontext/context.go` pass hardcoded selector literals (`"basePath"`, `"volumes"`), which makes correctness depend on exact ad-hoc strings.

`goseg/docker/events/events.go` and `goseg/broadcast/state.go` use fixed-size channels with default-case publish behavior; this low-level pattern can drop work under pressure rather than making backpressure policy explicit at call sites.

All reviewed files are concise package docs with domain-specific language rather than generic placeholder phrasing (e.g., ship lifecycle phases in goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go, StarTram error semantics in goseg/startram/doc.go).
No pass-through wrappers, boilerplate try/catch equivalents, debug log spam, or repetitive no-value comments appear in the assigned files.
Comments describe behavioral constraints and contract intent (ordering guarantees, normalized error identifiers) instead of restating syntax.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Package-level docs make strong claims but are not linked to inline code references/tests in these files, so future drift could introduce AI-style narrative debt without immediate detection.

evidence: No deprecated markers, migration TODO/FIXME notes, or legacy/new API coexistence hints are present in the five assigned files.
The docs present a single contract vocabulary (e.g., governed StarTram contract errors, strict upload action contract) without explicit dual-path migration language.
goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go points to a canonical governance document rather than temporary migration instructions.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: These files are documentation-only surfaces; they cannot prove whether migration shims or deprecated runtime paths still exist in implementation files outside this batch.

evidence: Assigned directories are large (`goseg/shipworkflow` 58 files, `goseg/config` 49, `goseg/docker/orchestration` 37), and the reviewed files in each directory mix distinct concerns (transport parsing, infra runtime wiring, filesystem bootstrap, context façade).

`goseg/docker/orchestration/container_bridge.go` is primarily container-runtime composition logic (imports `docker/orchestration/container`, `docker/registry`, `config`) but remains in the broad orchestration root instead of a focused container wiring package.

`goseg/config/config.go` is a context/state façade over `config/contextapi` and `config/startramstate`, while `goseg/config/bootstrap.go` handles startup filesystem/config persistence; both are placed in the same flat package despite different ownership boundaries.
`StartVersionSubsystem` in `goseg/routines/version.go` delegates to a blocking runner, while other `Start*` APIs in the batch are non-blocking, so naming no longer reliably signals call behavior.

`DockerData(pathType string)` in `goseg/defaults/defaults.go` uses opaque string selectors (`"volumes"` vs everything else), reducing intent clarity at call sites like `goseg/config/runtimecontext/context.go`.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Naming communicates domain intent in many places, but behavior-signaling prefixes/suffixes are not consistently trustworthy across runtime/startup APIs.

evidence: `runAutoVersionUpdateLoop` in `goseg/routines/version.go` suppresses first and second updater failures by design and only exits on the third; this is intentional but makes failure semantics less obvious at call sites.

`dockerSubscriptionHandlerWithRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` resets failure counters on unhandled actions, which can obscure whether transition workflow instability is persistent versus event-noise-driven.
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Control flow is mostly direct, but some resilience loops encode nuanced retry/window behavior without a single explicit policy surface.

evidence: Typed runtime seam structs (`workflowRuntime`, `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`) enforce many compile-time contracts across orchestration and workflow modules.

`PublishTransitionWithPolicy[T any]` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` accepts a `func(T)` publisher (no error return), which weakens compile-time enforcement for delivery-failure handling compared with `events.EventBroker` methods that return `error`.
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Most boundaries are typed, but one generic transition publishing seam erases failure signaling in type signatures.

evidence: `events.EventBroker` publish methods return `error` (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`), but multiple callers in `goseg/shipworkflow/newship.go` ignore those errors in `HandleNewShipBoot` and `ResetNewShip`.

`emitWorkflowTransitionResolved` in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` explicitly applies `transition.HandleTransitionPublishError`, showing a stricter contract than neighboring direct publish calls.

Mutable process-wide singleton runtimes exist in several modules: `defaultEventRuntime` (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`), `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` and loop controller (`goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`), and `defaultDockerSubsystemRuntime` (`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`).

Public setters (`SetDefaultEventRuntime`, `SetDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`, `SetDefaultBroadcastLoopController`) allow runtime behavior to change globally after startup, coupling behavior to initialization order and test sequencing.
Runtime config serialization uses mixed key conventions in the same struct (`CfgDir` tagged as `json:"CFG_DIR"` in `goseg/structs/configs.go`, while sibling fields use camelCase tags).
Patch/update plumbing normalizes around `cfgDir`/`CfgDir` in `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`, creating a compatibility island distinct from the persisted `CFG_DIR` shape.

`Start*` APIs have conflicting semantics across modules: `StartVersionSubsystem` and `StartBackupRoutinesWithContext` block (`goseg/routines/version.go`, `goseg/routines/system/system.go`), while `StartDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` is fire-and-return (`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`).

`DockerData(pathType string)` (`goseg/defaults/defaults.go`) exposes a stringly-typed selector contract where invalid values silently map to a fallback branch, making the API shape hard to use safely.
Leak broadcast path applies differentiated payload scoping by auth state (`status.Auth`) in `goseg/leak/broadcast.go`, preserving a clear authorized vs scoped-unauthorized split.
No contradictory authorization bypass pattern was observed in the sampled cross-cutting files; auth-sensitive publish pathways are mostly delegated to existing session/leak state.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Authorization behavior is mostly consistent in sampled files, but several transition/event publishers bypass explicit error handling, reducing observability if auth-gated deliveries fail.

findings.summary: Contract catalog entrypoints are inconsistent across sibling domains, blurring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `action/specs.go` requires callers to choose among multiple package-specific entrypoints (`ProtocolActionSpecs`, `UploadActionSpecs`, `UploadActionFamilySpecs`, `AllActionSpecs`) and types.
`startram/specs.go` uses a different single-entry function (`ContractSpecs`) with a different contract family shape, forcing package-specific knowledge for catalog aggregation.
Conformance tests must consume multiple binding/spec APIs, indicating no single obvious architectural source-of-truth surface for catalog discovery.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one canonical catalog aggregation interface at the contracts boundary (e.g., unified registry provider with typed adapters for payload-rule metadata) and make both action and startram packages implement that single shape.

findings.summary: Contract governance is duplicated across protocol conformance and upload service tests/docs.
evidence: `uploadsvc/doc.go` embeds cross-package governance policy text pointing to governance docs.
`conformance/fixtures_test.go` validates canonical action bindings and payload-rule exposure from the protocol side.
`uploadsvc/actions_test.go` separately asserts registry alignment and payload-rule validity for the same upload action contract set.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate governance-contract invariants into one protocol-level conformance suite and keep uploadsvc tests focused on service-specific parsing/validation behavior; expose shared conformance helpers to avoid repeating architectural invariants in multiple layers.

findings.summary: Mutable default event/runtime singletons can split producers and consumers onto different buses over process lifetime.
evidence: `DefaultEventRuntime()` returns the current runtime value, while `SetDefaultEventRuntime()` can replace channel sets later.
`newWorkflowRuntime()` captures `events.DefaultEventRuntime()` and builds a closure around it for transition publishing.
`defaultImporterRuntime()` wires `PublishImportTransitionFn` from `events.DefaultEventRuntime()` at construction time, so later default swaps do not rebind existing runtimes.
findings.suggestion: Make `EventBroker` an explicit dependency passed from a composition root and remove mutable global default swapping in runtime code paths; reserve global defaults for tests only behind explicit test helpers.

findings.summary: Oversized callback-bag runtimes create high seam entropy and obscure ownership of cross-module behavior.
evidence: Runtime structs contain large numbers of function fields and embedded groups (importer has multiple runtime group structs; startup orchestrator has layered init/background runtime groups; orchestration runtime ops expose broad mixed concerns).
Multiple `validate...Runtime` functions are needed per module to guard missing callbacks, indicating integration contracts are fragmented across many loosely related seams.
Cross-package orchestration depends on selecting and forwarding many callbacks instead of composing smaller role-focused collaborators.
findings.suggestion: Refactor callback bags into narrower role interfaces (for example: image selection, container lifecycle, transition publishing, config persistence) and inject only the collaborators each workflow step actually needs.

findings.summary: String-based selector APIs for runtime paths introduce brittle control flow and hidden invalid-state behavior.
evidence: `DockerData(pathType string)` checks `pathType == "volumes"` and uses an implicit else branch for all other values.
Callers use literal selector strings (`"basePath"` and `"volumes"`), so typo or drift changes behavior silently rather than failing fast.
`dockerDirDefault` in runtime context builds on this selector and appends `/`, coupling low-level path correctness to ad-hoc string conventions.
findings.suggestion: Replace `DockerData(string)` with explicit typed APIs (for example `DockerVolumesDir()` and `DockerBaseDir()`), and fail explicitly when runtime path discovery is unavailable.

findings.summary: Fixed-capacity channel publishers use immediate default-fail semantics, making overload behavior implicit and inconsistent across subsystems.
evidence: `publishDropOnFull` returns `ErrTransitionBusFull` via a non-blocking `default` case; events are dropped under transient backpressure.
`PublishSchedulePack` in broadcast runtime also uses a non-blocking send with `errSchedulePackBusFull`.
Docker subsystem listener returns an error when its event channel is saturated, turning pressure spikes into subsystem-level failures.
findings.suggestion: Define a shared backpressure policy (bounded wait with context, priority lanes for critical transitions, and overflow metrics) and apply it consistently across event and broadcast buses.

findings.summary: Container runtime wiring and type dispatch are misplaced in orchestration root
evidence: `container_bridge.go` defines `dockerContainerRuntimeInputs` and runtime assembly for Llama/Netdata/Minio, centered on container runtime composition.

`container_config.go` holds container-type-to-builder dispatch (`containerConfigBuilders`) and delegates to container-specific builders; concern affinity is container wiring, not generic orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/docker/orchestration/containerwiring/` for runtime bridge + config dispatch APIs; (2) move `container_bridge.go` first, then `container_config.go`, keeping exported wrappers in `orchestration` temporarily for compatibility; (3) update imports/usages via `rg -n "llamaRuntimeFromDocker|netdataRuntimeFromDocker|minioRuntimeFromDocker|ContainerConfigForType" goseg`; (4) validate with `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Ship workflow package owns both domain flow and infrastructure adapter composition
evidence: `newShipProvisioningRuntime()` wires infra dependencies directly (docker/network/config/event functions), effectively acting as an infrastructure composition root within a domain workflow package.
`HandleNewShipBoot` performs transport/action handling and emits websocket transition events while also launching provisioning, indicating mixed API/transport/domain orchestration responsibilities.
Parallel runtime-composition patterns exist in orchestration bridge files, reinforcing a repeated architectural split where composition roots are not centralized.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) add `goseg/shipworkflow/adapters/` (infra/runtime assembly) and `goseg/shipworkflow/transport/` (message/action handlers); (2) move runtime-builder logic from `newship.go` into adapters first, then move action-dispatch/boot handler logic from `newship_service.go` into transport; (3) keep thin compatibility wrappers in `shipworkflow` package during migration; (4) update imports using `rg -n "HandleNewShip|HandleNewShipBoot|ProvisionShip|newShipProvisioningRuntime" goseg`; (5) validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` and `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Config package mixes runtime context façade and bootstrap persistence responsibilities
evidence: `config.go` primarily proxies to `config/contextapi`/`config/startramstate` (runtime context and state façade APIs).
`bootstrap.go` manages startup lifecycle, config file creation/loading/persistence, and session-key setup.
The same package surface combines process-context mutation (`SetBasePath`, `SetDebugMode`) and bootstrapping I/O logic, which are distinct ownership boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/config/runtime/` for context façade APIs and `goseg/config/bootstrap/` for startup initialization flow; (2) move façade functions from `config.go` first and adapt callsites, then move initialization flow from `bootstrap.go` with tests; (3) leave temporary forwarding functions in `config` to reduce migration risk; (4) run import updates with `rg -n "SetBasePath|DebugMode|Initialize\(|ConfigFilePath|SessionKeyPath" goseg`; (5) validate via `go test ./goseg/config/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Contract-governance responsibilities are split across protocol and uploadsvc test surfaces.
evidence: README defines one backend contract gate command and names governance guarantees, but conformance logic is split between protocol/contracts and uploadsvc test files.
fixtures_test.go validates canonical action bindings and upload payload-rule exposure, while actions_test.go re-validates payload rules and registry alignment from uploadsvc.
This creates two ownership points for the same compatibility contract, increasing chance of drift when action contracts evolve.
findings.suggestion: Establish a single governance-owner test surface for cross-package contract conformance (prefer `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/`). Stage 1: move upload-contract conformance assertions from `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` into new focused files under `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/` (for example `upload_payload_rules_test.go` and `upload_registry_alignment_test.go`). Stage 2: keep `uploadsvc/actions_test.go` scoped to parser/runtime behavior only. Stage 3: update README and `goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go` to reference the canonical conformance package and remove duplicate ownership language. Validate with `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/conformance ./uploadsvc` then `go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`.

findings.summary: Contract policy, specs, and conformance checks are scattered across docs and package boundaries.
evidence: Policy entrypoints are split between README and package doc comments, while contract specs are in protocol catalogs and enforcement tests are split between protocol and uploadsvc.
Holistic structure data shows `goseg/protocol/contracts/` at 11 files with mixed production/test concerns, indicating broad folder scope and weaker navigability for governance tasks.
A contributor changing upload contract rules must traverse protocol catalog specs, protocol conformance fixtures, uploadsvc tests, and docs to confirm complete impact.
findings.suggestion: Reorganize governance assets into a staged structure. Stage 1 (target folders): keep contract declarations in `goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/*`, move all cross-package conformance tests into `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/` subfiles by domain (`actions_*`, `upload_*`, `startram_*`), and keep `goseg/uploadsvc/` tests runtime-only. Stage 2 (move order): (1) extract duplicated contract-conformance assertions from `uploadsvc/actions_test.go` into protocol conformance tests, (2) trim uploadsvc tests to parser/command validation, (3) consolidate governance prose to one canonical architecture doc referenced by README/doc.go. Stage 3 (import/update/validation): run `cd goseg && go test ./protocol/contracts/conformance -run Upload`, then `go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`, then full `go test ./...` to ensure moved tests preserve gate behavior.

findings.summary: `Start*` methods mix blocking and non-blocking semantics across sibling runtimes
evidence: `StartVersionSubsystem` delegates to `RunVersionSubsystemWithContext` and blocks until cancellation/worker failure.
`StartBackupRoutinesWithContext` starts goroutines but then blocks on `<-ctx.Done()`.
`StartDockerSubsystemWithRuntime` starts a goroutine and returns immediately, so the same `Start` prefix implies different lifecycle behavior.
findings.suggestion: Standardize lifecycle API shape across subsystems: either make `Start*` consistently non-blocking with paired `Run*`/`Wait*` APIs, or rename blocking starters to `Run*` and keep `Start*` for fire-and-return only.

findings.summary: Transition publish contract is inconsistently honored (errors handled in some paths, dropped in others)
evidence: `EventBroker.Publish*` methods return `error`, but `HandleNewShipBoot` and `ResetNewShip` call `PublishNewShipTransition` without checking returned errors.
`emitWorkflowTransitionResolved` routes publish errors through `transition.HandleTransitionPublishError`, showing a stricter contract in adjacent workflow code.
This creates split behavior where some transition failures are policy-managed and others are silently lost.
findings.suggestion: Route all event publishing through a single helper that enforces one error policy (critical/non-critical) and return/log outcomes consistently; update direct `DefaultEventRuntime().Publish*` call sites to use that helper.

findings.summary: Global mutable default runtimes create boot-order and test-order coupling
evidence: Each module exposes package-level default runtime state plus mutators (`SetDefault*`), allowing runtime behavior changes outside composition root.
`Start*` entrypoints often implicitly read defaults, so effective dependencies vary based on prior global mutations.
This pattern makes initialization order part of correctness and can cause cross-test leakage when defaults are replaced.
findings.suggestion: Move default-runtime wiring to explicit composition at startup (main/startup orchestrator) and pass runtime instances through constructors/functions; keep package globals only as backward-compatible shims that delegate to injected instances.

findings.summary: Runtime config uses mixed key casing conventions (`CFG_DIR` vs camelCase peers)
evidence: `RuntimeConfig.CfgDir` is tagged `json:"CFG_DIR"` while adjacent fields use camelCase tags (e.g., `updateInterval`, `binHash`).
Update registry surfaces `cfgDir`/`CfgDir` for patching, so runtime config key conventions differ between persistence and patch APIs.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a compatibility migration to canonicalize `CfgDir` to one key convention (prefer camelCase), accept legacy keys during read, and write only the canonical key to reduce long-term contract drift.
